scene.name: The Sunblasted Door, Where a World Was Drunk Dry

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Tamar
npcs.title: who gave away the water that was her life
npcs.hail: *(A lean, sun-dark woman sits in the shade of a dead tree beside an empty waterskin, and everything about her speaks of a world where the sky is an enemy and the ground gives nothing freely. Her skin is cracked from a heat you can almost feel coming off the memory of her; her lips are scarred from a lifetime of thirst held at bay by careful sips; and her hands, folded in her lap, have the particular economy of someone who learned young that every motion costs water you cannot spare. She comes, you understand, from a *burning* world — a place the wizards drank dry, where ages of greedy magic that fed on the living land itself had turned green fields to ash and seas to dust, until what remained was a sun-blasted waste where water was not a thing you drank but a thing you *were*, the one true wealth, hoarded and killed for and measured out in drops, in a world where to give away your water was, quite simply, to give away your life. And Tamar, you sense at once from the peace in her ruined face, did exactly that. She looks up at you with eyes that have squinted against an unforgiving sun for a lifetime, and there is no bitterness in them, only the deep quiet of a soul that spent the most valuable thing it owned and has never once regretted the spending — though it carries, you see, one small worry still.)* …A cold one. *(her voice is dry as the wind off a salt-pan)* …Crossed-over. You don't *thirst*, do you — I can see it, the dead don't want for water. *(a faint, cracked smile)* …Strange, to sit beside someone who isn't, every moment, doing the sums. How much is left. How far to the next. …Sit, cold one. …It's a rest I never had, the company of someone who isn't dying of want. …I'd like to feel it once.*

npcs.name: Kessa
npcs.title: a mage who would not burn the world for power
npcs.hail: *(A robed figure stands by a low brazier, and she is a sorcerer — you can feel the banked power in her, the long discipline — but she holds it the way one holds a thing one has chosen, repeatedly and at cost, not to misuse. She is from the burning world too, and she knows exactly how it burned, because she practiced the art at the heart of its ruin. For on that dying world, you sense, magic could be worked two ways: the slow, patient, *preserving* way, that drew its power gently and left the land alive — and the fast, ferocious, *defiling* way, that ripped the life out of the soil and the plants and everything green for a hundred paces around and *burned* it, instantly, into raw power, leaving a circle of grey ash and dead ground that would never grow again. The defilers were *mighty.* They rose fast, struck hard, became kings and tyrants and god-things on the stolen life of their own world — and each spell they cast killed a little more of the only world they had, until the whole of it was the grey waste it became. And Kessa learned both ways, and had the talent to be mighty, and *chose* — every spell, for a whole hard life — the slow way, the weak way, the way that left the ground alive, in a world where everyone who chose otherwise *won.* She regards you with a steady, weary, unbroken calm.)* …You have the look of a soul walking toward a hard choice. *(a quiet voice)* …I know that walk. I made a hard choice every time I raised my hand, for fifty years. …Sit, cold one. …Let me tell you about the kind of power that wins, and what it costs the world to let it.*

npcs.name: Oru
npcs.title: the last who remembers the world was green
npcs.hail: *(An ancient soul sits cross-legged at the foot of the milestone, so old and so weathered that at first you cannot guess what manner of being it was, only that it has carried something for a very, very long time. Its eyes are the thing that hold you: they are not the squinting sun-hardened eyes of the others, narrowed against a hostile sky — they are *soft*, and far away, and full of a green light that has no source anywhere in the grey world it came from, as though they are always, even now, looking at something the rest of that world forgot existed. For Oru is the last of a long line of rememberers — souls who took it upon themselves, generation after generation, to carry one thing the dying world had nearly lost entirely: the *memory* that it had once been *alive.* Green. Watered. Forested and flowered and full. The burning world Oru came from had been a waste for so many ages that almost no one living believed it had ever been anything else; the grey was simply *the world*, eternal, the way things had always been and always would be — and to the people scraping survival from the ash, the very idea that there had once been forests, rivers, rain, was a fairy-story, a cruelty, a thing not worth the breath. And Oru, and the rememberers before Oru, held the memory anyway — not because it helped, not because anyone thanked them for it, but because they understood that a world which forgets it was ever green can never, ever, want to be green again. Oru looks up at you, and the green light in those ancient eyes is the saddest and most hopeful thing you have seen in all this City.)* …Oh. *(a voice like wind through leaves that no longer exist)* …A traveler. …Come, sit by an old fool, and I will tell you a secret almost no one in my whole world believed: …it was *green*, once. …All of it. …And I am the last who still remembers …that it could be again.*

variants.text: *(She gestures to the shade beside her, an old desert courtesy — share the shadow, it costs nothing and may save a life — and folds her careful hands.)* …Sit in the shade, cold one. It's free. *(a dry, cracked smile)* …That's a strange thing to be able to say, where I'm from. Nothing's free, on the burning world. Not the shade, not the air hardly, and *never* the water. *(she touches the empty skin at her side)* …You have to understand about the water, cold one, or none of the rest of me will make sense. On my world, water wasn't a *drink.* It was *life*, made into a thing you could *hold* — the one wealth, the only one that mattered. Souls *killed* for a skinful. Whole *families* died for the want of what you'd spill from a cup without thinking. And the *rule* of it, the iron rule everyone learned at the breast: *your water is your life, and you do not give your life away.* *(she looks at her folded hands)* …I broke that rule, cold one. Once. All the way. *(quietly)* …There was a stranger, dying of thirst in the deep waste, where I'd no business being and neither did they. And I had water — not much, not enough for two, barely enough for *one* to reach the next well. …And I gave it to them. *All* of it. *(she meets your eyes, steady)* …And then I walked on toward the well I now could not reach, cold one, because the giving felt better than the arithmetic. …And I didn't make it. …And I have never, in all the time since, been *sorry.* *(the small worry flickers)* …Though there's a thing about it …I've never quite been able to set down. …Sit, and maybe you'll help me with it.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(She settles back against the dead tree, conserving herself out of an old habit her death has made unnecessary, and gives you her dry, steady attention.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a faint smile)* …I've time now, and no thirst pressing me to keep it short. That alone's a luxury I never had breathing. On the burning world you learn to say things in as few words as water allows. …It's a strange freedom, being able to *waste* a few.

choices.text: *(read the water-sharer — past the sun-scarred calm, to what it cost and what it meant)* "Let me look at you a moment, Tamar. A soul that gave away its own life in water — I'd see what that really was."
"Tell me about the stranger, Tamar — the one you gave your water to, and walked away from your own life for."

*(help her set down the worry that the stranger wasn't worth it — that generosity isn't a debt the recipient has to earn)* "That's the thing you can't set down, isn't it, Tamar — you don't know if the stranger *deserved* it. Whether they went on to be good, or wasted the life you bought them, or were a thief, a killer, nobody at all. Listen to me: it doesn't matter, and the not-mattering is the whole point. If you'd checked the stranger was *worthy* first — weighed them, made them earn it — then it wouldn't have been a gift. It would have been a *wage*, paid to someone who qualified. What you did was rarer and truer than that. You gave your life to a stranger *without* knowing if they'd earn it, because a soul dying of thirst doesn't need to be *good* to deserve water — it just needs to be *thirsty.* Your generosity wasn't a loan the stranger has to pay back by being worthwhile. It was complete the moment you gave it, and nothing they did after can reach back and make it less. You weren't a fool who gambled her life on an unworthy bet. You were the one soul on a whole burning world who understood that giving isn't conditional on the worth of who you give to — it's conditional on *nothing*, which is exactly what makes it the realest thing a soul can do."

*(the one who knows the Wall runs on the burning world's iron rule — and that giving is the only wealth that survives a dying world)* "Tamar — here's what your one broken rule taught you that my whole journey is about. Your world ran on an iron law: *your water is your life, and you do not give your life away.* Hoard. Take. Spend nothing on anyone. And that law is *exactly* the law of the Wall I'm walking toward — it prices every soul by what it's *owed*, what it can *keep*, what it can *take*, and it calls a soul that gave itself away for nothing a *fool* who failed to balance the books. But look what that law *built*, Tamar: it built your burning world. A whole planet of souls each hoarding their own water, each spending nothing on anyone, each surviving one more day at everyone else's expense — until the world itself was a grey waste of careful, frightened, perfectly *rational* hoarders dying alone with full skins. *That's* where the iron rule leads. And you broke it. You gave your water to a stranger, and you died, and in a world built entirely on *keeping*, you were the one soul who proved that *giving* was even possible. You weren't the failure of your world's law, Tamar. You were its one refutation. The hoarders all balanced their books and died on a dead world. You spent everything and died too — but you died having been, for one moment, *richer* than every king on that planet, because you owned the one wealth the burning world had forgotten existed: something worth more to you than your own survival."
"Rest in the shade, Tamar. I'll come back — and I'll not have decided your giving was a waste."

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(She holds still and lets you look, with the patience of someone who long ago stopped flinching from being seen, and you let your sense move past the sun-cracked calm to the soul that gave its life away in water.)* *(And your sense finds, first, that the giving was not impulsive — which is the thing that makes it remarkable. A sudden generosity, a thoughtless reflex, would be easier to understand and worth less; your sense reads instead that Tamar *did the arithmetic.* She knew, in the moment she unstoppered the skin, exactly what she was spending and exactly what it would cost — that there was not enough for two, that giving it away meant she would not reach the next well, that this was, in the plainest terms, a choice to die. She was not confused. She was not swept up. She counted the cost in full, in the hard currency of a world where water is life, and she paid it anyway, clear-eyed, for a stranger she did not know. And your sense reads *why*, and the why is the thing that undoes you, because it is not martyrdom and it is not despair and it is not a death-wish dressed as virtue. Your sense reads that Tamar simply could not do the other thing. That she stood in the deep waste with a dying stranger in front of her and her own life in a skin at her hip, and found that the version of herself who walked away with full water and a living stranger left dying behind her was a person she could not *be* — not because a rule forbade it, but because something in her was built so that another soul's thirst, right there, in front of her, weighed more than her own survival. Your sense reads that this is not weakness and not foolishness but a kind of wealth so rare the burning world had no word for it: a soul for whom giving was not a sacrifice grudgingly made but the only bearable option, the thing that *cost less* than the alternative of living with what she'd have had to become to keep her water. And your sense reads, last, the small worry she carries — not whether it was worth her life, she never doubts that, but whether it was worth it *to the stranger*, whether the life she bought was spent well or squandered — and reads that this worry is the last trace of the burning world's poison still in her, the lie that a gift's worth depends on the merit of who receives it, and that she is one true word away from being free of it.)*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, looking out at a waste only she can see, and when she speaks there's a faint wonder in it.)* …The stranger. *(softly)* …That's the thing, cold one — I never knew them. That's what everyone *fixed* on, after, the few who heard the story: *'but who* were *they, Tamar? Were they* worth *it? Was it someone* important?'* *(a dry, cracked laugh)* …As though it would have been a *better* death if the stranger turned out to be a *prince*, or a *worse* one if they were a *nobody.* …I'll tell you what I knew of them, cold one. *Everything* I knew, which took about a breath to learn. *(she holds up sun-dark fingers, counting)* …I knew they were on the ground. I knew they were not yet dead. I knew their lips were black and their skin had stopped sweating, which on the burning world means you have *hours*, maybe less. And I knew — *(she meets your eyes)* …I knew the sound a soul makes when it has given up, cold one. That low, dry, *done* sound. They were making it. *(quietly)* …That's *all* I knew. Not their name. Not their people. Not whether they were *good* or *wicked* or *clever* or *kind.* …I had a *skin* of water and a *stranger* making the *done* sound, and a choice. …And do you know what I have *come* to think, cold one, in all the time since? *(softly)* …I think *not* knowing them was the *gift's* whole *grace.* …If I'd *known* them — known they were *good*, *deserving*, *worthy* — then giving them my water would've been a *trade*, a thing they'd *earned.* …But I *didn't* know them. They were just *thirst*, in the *shape* of a *person.* …And I gave my *life* to *thirst-in-the-shape-of-a-person*, cold one, *because* it was *thirsty* — *(barely)* …and *that* …I have come to think *that* is the only *pure* thing I ever *did.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(You don't tell her the stranger was surely worthy — you tell her that worth was never the question, that a gift weighed against the merit of its receiver stops being a gift at all, and the truth of it reaches the one place in her the sun never cracked. She goes very still, and then her sun-dark face crumples, and she presses a scarred hand to her mouth.)* …*'A soul dying of thirst doesn't need to be good to deserve water. It just needs to be thirsty.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. *(she has to gather herself)* …That is the — that is the thing, the *exact* thing, I have turned over a *thousand* times in the dark and never once got *right.* *(her voice fills)* …I kept *checking*, you see. In my *mind.* I'd *imagine* the stranger *lived* — went on, did *good*, became someone *worth* the *water* — and I'd feel *easy.* And then I'd imagine them a *thief*, a *killer*, someone who *wasted* the *years* I *bought* — and the *worry* would come *back*, the *'then it was for* nothing, *Tamar, you* died *for* nothing.'* *(she shakes her head slowly, wonderingly)* …And you've just shown me I was *asking* the *burning* *world's* *question.* …*'Did they* earn *it.'* …As though my *giving* were a *loan*, and the stranger's *whole* *life* after was the *repayment*, and if they *defaulted* then I'd been a *fool.* *(fiercely now, through tears)* …But it was *never* a *loan.* …It was a *gift.* …And a *gift's* worth doesn't *depend* on the one who *takes* it — it's *complete* in the *giving.* *(she presses both hands to her chest)* …The stranger could be the *worst* soul who ever *drew* breath, cold one, and it *would* not *reach* *back* and make my *giving* *less*, because I didn't give it to their *worth.* …I gave it to their *thirst.* …And their *thirst* was *real.* *(she breathes, and a weight an age old lifts off her)* …I can set it *down* now. …The *worry.* …It was the *last* of the *burning* *world* still *in* me — the *idea* that *love* has to be *deserved.* …And you've — *(barely, free)* …you've *poured* it *out*, cold one. …Like *water* …for a *stranger.* …*Thank* you.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(You tell her that the stranger's worth was never the measure, and she listens with her careful stillness, and something in her loosens — the grip of an old question easing, though not all at once.)* …Worth was never the question. *(slowly)* …You say it plain, cold one, and there's — there's something in it that lands true. *(she turns her empty waterskin over)* …I *did* keep asking the wrong thing. *'Were they worth it.'* …And you're saying that to even *ask* it is to turn the gift into a *trade.* *(a slow breath)* …I'll sit with that. It's a deep old splinter, and it doesn't draw in one pull. *(she meets your eyes, and there's the first ease in them)* …But you've got hold of it, cold one — the *right* end of it, which is more than I managed in all this time. *'I gave it to their thirst, not their worth.'* …Come back, and say it to me again, would you? *(quietly)* …The burning world taught me, *bone-deep*, that *nothing's* free, that *everything's* a *trade*, that you don't *give* what isn't *earned.* …It'll take more than one telling to *unlearn* a whole *world.* …But you've *started* it, cold one. …For the first time, the worry feels like a thing I *could*, *someday*, set *down.* …That's further than I've been …in a *long* *while.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(You mean to lift the worry — but you do it by reassuring her the stranger surely lived well, surely deserved it, and she gives you a tired, gentle smile, because you have answered the wrong question and confirmed, without meaning to, the very frame that traps her.)* …You're trying to tell me they were *worth* it. *(quietly)* …That they *lived*, did *good*, *earned* the water. *(she shakes her head, not unkind)* …Cold one, you can't *know* that — and the *trouble* is, neither can *I*, and *that's* the whole *cage* I'm *in.* If my *peace* depends on the stranger having turned out *worthy*, then I'll *never* have it, because I'll *never* *know.* *(she touches the empty skin)* …You've tried to *settle* the *question* — *'they were worth it, Tamar, rest easy'* — when what I need is for someone to tell me the *question* *itself* is *wrong.* *(quietly)* …Don't *answer* whether they *deserved* it, cold one. …Tell me whether *deserving* was ever the *point.* *(she meets your eyes)* …Come back when you can do *that.* …'Cause as long as you're *reassuring* me the stranger was *good*, you're *agreeing* with the part of me that thinks my *water* was only *well-spent* if they *earned* it. …And *that* part, cold one — *(barely)* …that part is the burning world *talking.* …I need someone to talk *louder* than my *world.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(It comes out wrong — you try to comfort her by saying it doesn't matter if the stranger was unworthy, because the gesture made *her* noble, made *her* good — and she draws back, because you have made her death about *her own* virtue, turned the stranger into a mere prop for Tamar's goodness, which is the one thing the gift was never about.)* …No. *(quietly, with an edge)* …No, cold one. You've turned it *backwards.* *(she sits up)* …You're saying it doesn't *matter* if the stranger was *worthy*, because giving made *me* *noble* — that the *whole* thing was really about *my* *goodness*, and the stranger was just the — the *occasion* for it. *(she shakes her head, hard)* …That's *worse* than the *worry*, cold one. That makes the stranger a *thing* I *used* to *prove* I was *good.* *(her voice tightens)* …I didn't give my water to *become* *noble.* I didn't give it for *me* at all. I gave it because there was a *soul* *dying* in front of me and I *couldn't* *walk* *past.* …The *stranger* wasn't a *prop* for *Tamar's* *virtue*, cold one. …The stranger was the *whole* *reason*, a *real* *person*, *thirst* in the *shape* of someone who *mattered* *in* *themselves*, not because they let *me* be *good.* *(she looks away)* …*Go.* …And don't come *back* telling me my death was a fine thing because it made *me* shine. …That's just the burning world's *selfishness* wearing a *kinder* *coat* — *still* making it all about *what I got*, even if what I *got* was *virtue* instead of *water.* …Come back when you understand: …it was *never* about *me.* …It was about the *stranger* being *worth* *giving* to …*simply* *because* they were *thirsty.* …*That's* the thing. …Not my *nobility.* …*Their* *thirst.**

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(She listens, and as you speak her sun-dark face moves through something profound — recognition, and grief, and at last a fierce, dry-eyed pride she has never let herself feel. By the end she is sitting straight against the dead tree, and her cracked voice has iron in it.)* …*'The hoarders all balanced their books and died on a dead world.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. *(she presses a hand flat against the dead earth beside her)* …You have *named* my whole *world.* …That's *exactly* what it *was* — a planet of *careful*, *rational*, *frightened* souls, each one *hoarding* their own water, each *spending* *nothing* on *anyone*, each *surviving* one more *day* at everyone *else's* cost — and *every* one of them *right*, by the *iron* *rule*, *right* to *keep*, *right* to *take*, *right* to *give* *nothing* away. *(her voice fills)* …And the *iron* *rule* *built* a *grey* *waste* full of *full* *skins* and *dead* *souls.* …Because a *world* where *everyone* *keeps* *everything* …*is* a *dead* *world*, cold one. The *keeping* *is* the *dying.* *(she rises, lean and fierce against the dead tree)* …And your *Wall.* *(quietly, certain)* …You've shown me what your Wall *is.* It's the *iron* *rule*, *cold one*, *made* into *law* and *handed* a *ledger.* *'Your worth is what you're owed. Hoard it. Take. Give nothing away you won't get back. Balance the books.'* *(fiercely)* …It is the *defiler's* *rule.* The *hoarder's* *rule.* The rule that *drank* my *world* *dry.* …And *every* soul mortared in that *Wall* for having *given* *itself* *away* and *gotten* *nothing* *back* — *(she grips your arm, sun-dark fingers strong)* …every soul that *spent* its *water* on a *stranger* and *died* of it — *they* are not the *Wall's* *failures*, cold one. …They are the *only* *ones* who ever *broke* the *rule* that *kills* *worlds.* *(blazing)* …*Go.* …Go to your *Wall*, cold one, and tell the *hoarders* who *built* it the thing my *whole* *dead* *world* *learned* too *late*: …*the souls who gave everything away are the only wealth a dying world has left.* …The *keepers* *balanced* their *books* …and *the* *world* *died* *anyway.* …The *givers* *spent* it *all* …and were the *only* *rich* *ones* *on* the *whole* *grey* *planet.* …*Pour* *yourself* *out*, cold one. …It's the only thing …that was *ever* *worth* *anything.*

nodes.speaker: Tamar
nodes.text: *(She nods, and settles back into the free shade, and there is a new ease in the set of her shoulders.)* …Go on, cold one. Thank you for sitting where it costs nothing. *(she nods toward Kessa at the brazier)* …The mage there — *Kessa* — she's the other half of my story, though she'd put it more cleverly. *I* gave away my *water*; *she* gave away her *power* — refused the magic that would've made her a *queen*, because it *burned* the *world* to *cast* it. *(quietly)* …Same choice, cold one, hers and mine. *Spend* yourself, or *spend* the *world.* …She chose like I did. *Go* hear it from her — she understands the *why* of it better than I ever could; I just *did* it, she can *explain* it. *(and then her sun-dark face goes gentle, looking toward Oru)* …And the *old* one. By the *stone.* *(softly)* …*Oru* remembers when my world was *green*, cold one. *Before* the *defilers* *drank* it *dry.* …I never *saw* it green. *No* one *living* did. …But *Oru* *holds* it — the *memory* that it *was*, and *could* be *again* — and that, cold one — *(barely)* …that is the only thing on the whole *burning* *world* …that was ever *worth* more than *water.* …*Go* to *Oru*, cold one. …*Last*. …And let the *old* one *show* you …what we were all *dying* *for.**

conversations.title: Kessa, Who Would Not Burn the World

variants.text: *(She lowers her hand from the brazier and turns to you fully, and the banked power in her is like standing near a closed furnace — you feel the heat of what she could do, held behind the door of what she chose.)* …Sit, cold one. I'll not waste your time with mysteries; I dealt in hard truths all my life and I've no patience left for anything else. *(a steady, weary voice)* …I am a sorcerer of the burning world. And you should understand what that *meant* there, because it is not what it means elsewhere. On my world, cold one, magic had a *price*, and the price was *paid by the land.* *(she gestures at the dead ground)* …There were two ways to work it. The slow way — the *preserving* way — drew power gently, a little at a time, left the soil alive, the plants standing, and made you *weak*, and *patient*, and *poor*, in a world that *ate* the weak. *(her jaw tightens)* …And the fast way. The *defiling* way. You reached into the living land — the grass, the roots, the slow green patience of a place — and you *ripped* the *life* out of it, all at once, and *burned* it into power, and where you stood became a circle of grey ash that would *never* grow again. *(quietly)* …The defilers were *mighty*, cold one. They became *kings.* *God-things.* They *won*, every one of them, fast and high — and each spell they cast *killed* a little more of the only world we had, until the *whole* of it was the ash you see me sitting in. *(she meets your eyes, unflinching)* …I had the talent to be *mighty*, cold one. I knew *both* ways. …And I chose the *weak* one. *Every* spell. For *fifty* *years.* …Sit, and I'll tell you what that cost — and why I'd pay it again.*

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(She folds her hands into her sleeves, the banked power quieting, and regards you with the steady patience of someone who has explained hard things to people who did not want to hear them and learned to do it without heat.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a quiet voice)* …I'll give you the truth of it, even the parts that don't flatter me. *Especially* those. A soul that spent fifty years refusing an easy power learns there's no point lying about how much the refusing hurt.

choices.text: *(read the preserver — past the weary calm, to the fifty-year discipline of refusing power that would have made her a queen)* "Hold still a moment, Kessa. Let me look — at the cost of refusing, every spell, for fifty years."
"What was the temptation, Kessa? Truly. Fifty years with an easier, mightier power right there in reach — what kept your hand off it?"

*(the one who knows the Wall is the defiler's bargain — power now, paid for by a future that never gets to vote)* "Kessa — your two kinds of magic are the truest thing I've found for what I'm walking toward. Defiling: reach into the living future, rip the life out of it, burn it into power *now*, and leave grey ash that never grows again. That's not just your world's curse. That's the *logic of the Wall* I'm facing — and the logic of every power that ever called itself necessary. Spend the future to be mighty in the present. Take what isn't yours to take, because it's *there*, and it *works*, and the cost falls on someone who isn't in the room to object — the soil, the unborn, the world that comes after. The defiler's whole bargain is that the bill goes to a future that doesn't get to vote. And you, Kessa — for fifty years, weak and poor and passed over while the defilers became kings — you *refused to send that bill.* You paid, yourself, now, in your own strength and comfort and standing, rather than charge it to a world that couldn't speak for itself. My Realm's Wall is the greatest defiler of all: it burns the worth of the unclaimed dead to fuel the order of the powerful, and calls the ash *justice.* You're the proof the other way exists — that a soul can decline to be mighty on stolen life, and leave the ground alive behind it. The defilers won, and inherited a corpse. You lost, and kept faith with a world you'd never live to see green. Only one of those is actually a victory."
"What did it cost you, Kessa — really? Staying weak in a world that ate the weak, while lesser souls who chose the easy power became kings?"
"Keep your fire banked, Kessa. I'll come back — I've a power of my own I'm deciding whether to spend."

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(She inclines her head and lets you look, the banked power going still, and you let your sense move past the weary calm to the fifty-year discipline beneath it.)* *(And your sense finds that the refusal was never easy — that this is the thing she most wants understood and most rarely is. People imagine the preserver as a serene soul above temptation, naturally gentle, for whom declining the defiling power was no struggle because she simply didn't *want* it. Your sense reads the opposite. Kessa wanted it. Your sense finds, fifty years deep, the wanting undimmed: every time she worked the slow patient weak magic and watched it *fail* to be enough — watched the defilers blast their enemies to ash and rise to thrones while she scraped by, watched people she could have *protected* suffer because her preserving power was *too weak* to save them, watched the easy mighty answer sit there in reach the whole time — your sense reads how badly she wanted to *reach* for it, how reasonable it would have been, how many times she had the spell half-formed in her mind, the one that would rip the life from the ground and make her, finally, *strong enough.* And every time, your sense reads, she stopped her own hand — not because she didn't want the power, but because she had understood a thing the defilers refused to: that the power was *real*, and *worked*, and the ash was *also* real, and *permanent*, and that to take the one was to make the other, forever, with no taking-back. Your sense reads the particular loneliness of her discipline: that a defiler's victories were *visible* — thrones, armies, blasted enemies, the whole world able to see what the power bought — while Kessa's restraint was *invisible*, fifty years of a thing *not done*, a world *not killed* a little further, ground *left alive* that no one would ever know she'd spared because the not-killing left no monument. She saved a thousand small futures that will never thank her, because a future you preserve does not know it was ever in danger. And your sense reads, last, the doubt she has carried — whether her restraint was *self-indulgence*, whether keeping her own hands clean while the weak she might have saved with stolen power suffered was a *vanity*, a way of valuing her own purity over other people's lives. Your sense reads that this is the hardest question a preserver faces and that Kessa faced it honestly every day, and chose, every day, to believe that a world kept alive was worth more than any soul she could have saved by helping kill it — and that she is not entirely sure she was right, and has been waiting a long time for someone to tell her she was.)*

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(She is quiet, and the banked power seems to pulse once, as if the question stirred it.)* …The temptation. *(a long breath)* …Cold one, let me dispel the *pretty* version first, the one people *want* to be true: that I refused the defiling power because I was *good*, because I found it *distasteful*, because some pure soul in me *recoiled.* *(a dry, hard sound)* …No. I *wanted* it. *Every* day. *(quietly)* …You have to understand *when* the wanting was *worst.* It wasn't when I was *ambitious* — I could refuse it easily enough when all it offered was a *throne*; I didn't *want* a throne. *(her jaw tightens)* …It was worst when the *weak* magic *failed* *someone.* …There was a *village*, cold one. Raiders. I stood between them and the people, and I worked the *preserving* way, the *slow* way, the *only* way that *leaves* the *world* *alive* — and it was *not* *enough.* I was *not* *strong* *enough.* And people *died*, cold one, in front of me, that a *single* *defiling* *spell* would have *saved* — one *circle* of *ash*, one *patch* of *ground* *killed*, and those *people* *live.* *(her voice is very steady, which is how you know what it costs)* …*That* is the temptation, cold one. Not *power* for its *own* sake. *Power* to *save* the *person* *bleeding* in *front* of you, *right* *now*, paid for by a *world* that *isn't* in the *room.* …And do you know what *kept* my *hand* *off* it, in *that* *moment*, with the *people* *dying?* *(barely)* …I *looked* at the *ground.* The *living* *ground.* And I thought of *every* defiler who'd *stood* *exactly* *here*, with *exactly* this *reason* — *'just* *this* *once*, for *something* that *matters*, just *this* *one* *patch* of *world' — *and I saw that the *whole* *grey* *waste* of my *entire* *planet* …was *made* of *that* *exact* *thought*, a *billion* times over. *Every* defiler had a *village.* …The *world* *died*, cold one, *one* *justified* *circle* of *ash* at a *time.* …So I let the people die, and kept the ground alive, and I have *never* been *sure* I was right …and I would *do* it *again.* …Because *someone* has to be the one …who *won't* *light* the *match*, …*even* for a *good* *reason.* …*Especially* for a good reason. …The *bad* reasons never *burned* much. …It was the *good* ones that *killed* the *world.**

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(For a long moment she does not move, and then the banked power in her seems to *settle*, to bank lower and steadier, the way a fire does when a long vigil over it is finally shared. When she speaks the weariness has not left her voice, but something under it has straightened.)* …*'The bill goes to a future that doesn't get to vote.'* *(quietly)* …Cold one. *(she presses her fingertips together)* …Fifty years I worked the slow magic, and *fifty* years I could not have told you, in a *sentence*, *why* it mattered — only that it *did*, that the *ash* was *wrong*, that something in the *defiling* was a *theft* even when no one *living* was *robbed.* *(her voice fills)* …And you have *named* the victim. …The *future.* …The one party to every defiler's bargain that is *never* *in* the *room*, never *consulted*, never *gets* to *say* *no* — the *world* that *comes* *after*, the *unborn*, the *green* that *would* have *grown* on the *ground* I'd have *burned.* *(she nods slowly, fiercely)* …*That* is the *theft.* …Defiling is *robbing* a *soul* that *cannot* *defend* *itself* *because* it *does* *not* *exist* *yet.* …And the *whole* *grey* *waste* of my *world* is what a *planet* looks like …when *every* generation *votes* to *rob* the *next* one. *(and the weary eyes come up, and they are clear)* …And your *Wall*, cold one. *(quietly, with the certainty of a thing long suspected and finally proven)* …You are right. It is the *greatest* *defiler* of *all.* …It takes the *worth* of the *unclaimed* *dead* — souls who can no longer *speak*, *futures* that will never *come* — and it *burns* them into the *order* of the *powerful*, the *comfort* of the *living*, the *stability* of the *now* — and it calls the *ash* *justice.* *(fiercely)* …It is *defiling* *dressed* as *law.* …*Spend* the *helpless* to *fuel* the *mighty*, and call the *grey* *waste* you *make* *order.* *(she rises, the banked power rising with her, but held, always held)* …*Go* and *refuse* it, cold one. The way I refused the *easy* spell with the *village* *dying.* …It will *cost* you. The *defiler's* way *always* *looks* *stronger*, *always* *wins* the *visible* *fight* — and your *Wall* will *offer* you, I have *no* *doubt*, some *mighty* *reason* to let it *stand*, some *village* it's *protecting*, some *good* it *does.* *(she meets your eyes)* …*Don't* *light* the *match*, cold one. …*Even* for the *good* *reason.* …*Especially* then. …*Leave* the *ground* *alive.* …The *defilers* *won* every *fight* …and *inherited* a *corpse.* …*Be* the *one* …who *loses* the *fight* …and *keeps* the *world.**

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(She looks down at her own hands, the hands that could have called down ruin and never did, and her weary voice goes quiet.)* …What it cost. *(a slow breath)* …*Everything* the defilers *got*, cold one — that was the cost. Every *throne* I *didn't* sit. Every *enemy* I *couldn't* *blast.* Every *year* I spent *weak*, and *poor*, and *passed* *over*, *watching* souls with a *tenth* of my *talent* and *none* of my *restraint* rise to become *kings* and *god-things* on the *stolen* *life* of the *world*, while I — *(a dry sound)* …while I *scraped*, and *struggled*, and *failed* to *save* people I *could* have *saved*, if only I'd been *willing* to *pay* for it with *ground* instead of *blood.* *(she meets your eyes)* …That's the *visible* cost, cold one. The one *anyone* could *see.* …But I'll tell you the one that *cut* *deepest*, the one *no* one *saw.* *(quietly)* …*No* one *knew.* …That's the *cost* of *preserving*, cold one — it is *invisible.* When a *defiler* *blasts* an *army*, the *whole* *world* *sees* the *power*, *names* it, *fears* it, *remembers* it. When *I* *spared* a *patch* of *living* *ground* — *declined*, *quietly*, to *kill* a *little* *more* of the *world* — *nothing* *happened.* No *monument.* No *song.* The ground I *saved* didn't *know* it was *saved.* The *futures* I *kept* *alive* will *never* *know* they were ever in *danger.* *(barely)* …I spent *fifty* *years*, cold one, *saving* things that would *never* *thank* me, by *not* *doing* a thing *no* one would ever *know* I *hadn't* *done.* …The defiler gets a *kingdom* and the *world* *remembers* his *name.* …The preserver gets *grey* *ground* that *stayed* *green* a *little* *longer* …and *no* one, *ever*, *knows* her *name* at *all.* *(she folds her hands)* …*That's* the cost, cold one. …Not the *weakness.* …The *invisibility* of *restraint.* …The way the *world* can *only* *see* the *match* being *struck* …and *never* the *thousand* *times* you *chose* …*not* to *strike* it.*

nodes.speaker: Kessa
nodes.text: *(She nods, and banks the power lower, and there is the ghost of approval in her weary face.)* …Go, cold one. And *think* on it — *truly* — that power of yours you're deciding whether to spend. *(quietly)* …Most powers offer you the *defiler's* bargain without ever *naming* it. *'Be* *mighty* *now.* *Someone* *else*, *somewhere* *else*, *later*, *pays.'* …*Ask*, *always*, *who* *isn't* in the *room.* *(she nods toward Oru at the milestone)* …And go to the *old* one. *Oru.* *(her voice softens, the first time it has)* …I spent fifty years keeping the ground *alive* for a *green* I would *never* *see* — and there were *days*, cold one, *hard* *days*, when I *doubted* the green had *ever* been *real*, when I thought I was *starving* myself for a *fairy-story.* *(barely)* …*Oru* is the one who kept the *fairy-story* *true.* The *last* who *remembers* the world was *green*, who *holds* the *picture* of the *thing* I was *dying* to *protect.* *(quietly)* …A *preserver* *without* a *rememberer*, cold one, is just a *fool* *starving* for *nothing.* …It was souls like *Oru* who made my *restraint* make *sense* — who *held* the *vision* of *what* the *ground* was *for.* …*Go* and let *Oru* *show* you. …And then you'll *understand* …what *Tamar* *died* for, and what *I* *starved* for, and what your *whole* *journey* is *toward*: …not just *stopping* the *burning.* …*Remembering* it could be *green* *again.**

variants.text: *(The ancient soul pats the ground beside it in invitation, and as you settle the green light in its eyes seems to deepen, as though your attention gives the old memory more room to breathe.)* …Sit, traveler, sit. *(a voice like wind through long-gone leaves)* …You are kind to listen to an old rememberer. There are not many of us left — there were *never* many; it is not a calling that *feeds* you — and I am, I think, the *last.* *(it folds its weathered hands)* …I will tell you what I am, traveler, and you must not laugh, though on my world they did. …I *remember.* That is my whole craft, my whole life's work, passed to me from the rememberer before, as she had it from the one before her, back and back, in an unbroken chain across all the long grey ages: …I remember that the world was *green.* *(its far eyes shine)* …You cannot imagine, traveler, how *mad* that sounds, on the burning world. The grey waste is so *total*, so *old*, that to the souls scraping life from the ash, the green is not *history* — it is *heresy.* A *lie.* A *cruelty.* *'The world was always grey. It is the nature of the world to be grey. Stop telling children it was ever otherwise; you only make the ash harder to bear.'* *(quietly)* …And we held the memory *anyway.* Generation on generation. The *forests.* The *rivers.* The *rain* — oh, traveler, the *rain* — passed down in *words*, because there was nothing *left* to *show* them. *(it meets your eyes)* …Sit, and I will tell you *why* we bothered. …Why a soul would *starve* to remember a *green* …that *no* one *living* would *ever* *see.**

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(It settles, the green light steady in its ancient eyes, with the gentle patience of a soul whose entire vocation was outlasting the forgetting.)* …Ask, traveler. *(a soft, rustling voice)* …I have answered these questions only to the rememberer who came after me, all my life — there was no one else who wished to know. It is a strange sweetness, to be asked by a stranger. …Go on.

choices.text: *(read the rememberer — past the ancient frailty, to the thing it has carried unbroken across the grey ages)* "Let me look at you a moment, Oru. The last who holds the memory of a living world — I'd see what that is."
"Show me, then, Oru. The green. Tell it the way you'd tell the next rememberer — make me see the world the way it was."

*(the one who knows a memory of green is a seed — that a world which remembers it was alive can want to be alive again)* "Oru — I think I finally understand why the rememberers starved for this, and it's the most important thing in all these doors. You said it yourself, without quite hearing it: *a world which forgets it was ever green can never want to be green again.* That's not a small thing. That's *everything.* The defilers didn't just burn your world's forests — the worst thing they did was make the grey seem *eternal*, *natural*, the way things had *always* been, because a soul that believes the waste is simply *how the world is* will never lift a finger to change it. Despair isn't sad, Oru — it's *useful*, to whatever made the waste. And the one thing despair cannot survive is the memory that it was *once otherwise.* You weren't keeping a fairy-story alive. You were keeping alive the single fact that makes renewal *possible*: that the grey is not the world's nature but its *wound* — and a wound, unlike a nature, can heal. A memory of green is a *seed*, Oru. It's the most subversive thing on a dead world: the stubborn refusal to let the ash call itself eternal. My Realm has a Wall that wants exactly what the defilers wanted — for souls to believe its grey logic is simply *how things are*, fixed, natural, forever. And the most dangerous thing a soul can carry into a place like that is your exact craft: the unkillable memory that it was, that it could be, *green.*"
"Can it ever come back, Oru? Truly? Or is the green just a thing to grieve — a world that's gone and isn't coming?"
"Hold the green a while longer, Oru. I'll come back — and now there are two of us who'll remember it."

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(It holds still, the green light in its eyes steadying, and you let your sense move past the ancient frailty into the thing it has carried unbroken across the grey ages.)* *(And your sense finds a vault. That is the only word for what is inside Oru: a vast, lovingly-tended, perfectly-preserved vault, holding a world that no longer exists. Your sense reads the contents and they are staggering in their specificity — not a vague green blur, but *detail*, held with the ferocious precision of people who knew that a memory loses its power the moment it goes soft: the exact sound of rain on broad leaves, the names of forty trees that no longer grow anywhere, the way a particular river smelled in spring, the color of a flower extinct ten thousand years, the feel of *mud* — mud! on the burning world! — between the toes of a child who has been dust since before the oldest living soul's grandmother was born. Your sense reads the *chain*, too: that this vault was not Oru's alone but was handed down, rememberer to rememberer, each one receiving the whole green world in words and holding it whole and passing it whole to the next, an unbroken relay across more ages than your sense can compass, every link a soul who chose to spend its life memorizing a paradise it would never see rather than let the last thread to it snap. And your sense reads the *cost* — that Oru is right, it does not feed you, this craft; that every rememberer starved a little, was thought mad, was told to let it go, *'why torment yourself and everyone else with a green that's gone'* — and held on anyway. And your sense reads, deepest of all, *why*, and it is not nostalgia and not grief, though both are in it. It is *defiance.* Your sense reads that the rememberers understood, with perfect clarity, the one thing the defilers and the despairing did not: that the most permanent victory of any ruin is to make itself seem *eternal*, to erase the memory of the before so completely that no one can even *imagine* an after — and that as long as one soul, even one, still holds the true memory that the world was *green*, the grey can never finish its conquest, because it can never become the *only* thing that was ever thinkable. Oru is not mourning a dead world. Your sense reads it true: Oru is the last sentry of the possibility that it could live again, holding a seed across the dark, refusing to let the ash declare itself the end of the story.)*

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(Its ancient face transforms, the green light flooding its eyes, and the rustling voice fills with a love so old and so practiced it has become a kind of music.)* …The green. *(a breath like wind in leaves)* …Close your eyes, traveler — no, truly, a rememberer always tells it so — and I will give it to you the way it was given to me, and to the one before me, back to the souls who *saw* it with living eyes. *(softly)* …There was *rain.* …You do not know what that *word* meant, on my world; we said it like a *prayer*, a thing so far gone it was almost holy. Water that fell *from the sky.* *Free.* On *everyone.* The rich and the poor *alike*, traveler, *drenched* in the one wealth our whole grey age would *kill* for, falling *freely* from *clouds* — and the people *did not even look up*, because there was *so much* of it that it was *ordinary.* *(it laughs, wondering, grieving)* …Imagine *that.* A world so *rich* in *life* that *water* was *boring.* *(its voice deepens)* …And the *green* itself — *forests*, traveler, which were *not* a *few* *guarded* *trees* behind a *warlord's* *wall* but *whole* *horizons* of them, more trees than a soul could *count* in a *lifetime*, so many that *wood* was something you *burned* *without* *thinking*, that *birds* — there were *birds*, traveler, *clouds* of them — lived in by the *thousand.* …And under the trees, *cool.* *Shade* that was not *rationed.* *Moss.* *Streams* you could drink from *cupped* *hands*, *as much as you wanted*, and *no* one would *kill* you for it. *(its far eyes shine with tears)* …And *mud*, traveler. …The thing I *return* to, always, last and best. …*Mud.* Wet *earth*, between a *child's* *toes*, *squelching*, and the child *laughing* because it was *dirty* and *cool* and *alive*, on a world so *drowning* in *water* that *getting* *muddy* was a thing a child could do *for* *fun.* *(barely)* …*That* was my world, traveler. …*That.* …And they *burned* it …for *power.* …And *almost* *everyone* *forgot* …it was *ever* …anything …but *grey.* *(it opens its eyes, and the green light is fierce now)* …But *I* did not forget. …*Say* it back to me, traveler, that you *heard* it. …The *rain.* The *forests.* The *mud.* …Now there are *two* of us …who *hold* it *true.**

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(The ancient soul goes utterly still, and the green light in its eyes blazes up brighter than you have yet seen it, and when it speaks the rustling voice trembles with the force of a thing it has known in its bones for an age and never once heard named aloud.)* …*'A memory of green is a seed.'* *(barely)* …Traveler. *(it presses a weathered hand to the dead ground)* …In all the long ages of the rememberers — in the whole unbroken chain of us, back to the ones who *saw* the green with *living* *eyes* — *no* one ever told us *why* it *mattered.* We *held* the memory because we were *given* it, because *letting* it *fall* felt like a *death* — but *why*, traveler, *why* it was *worth* a *life* of *starving* and being *thought* *mad* — *that* we *never* had the *words* for. *(its voice fills)* …And you have *given* them. …*'A world which forgets it was ever green can never want to be green again.'* …*'Despair isn't sad — it's* useful, *to whatever made the waste.'* *(it nods, fierce and weeping)* …*Yes.* …*That* is what we *were* *doing*, all those ages, without *knowing* it. …We were *not* *mourning.* …We were *keeping* the *one* *thing* the *grey* could *not* *abide*: …the *knowledge* that it was *not* *eternal.* …That it was a *wound*, and *not* a *nature.* …And a *wound* *can* *heal.* *(it rises, ancient and unbent, the green light streaming)* …And your *Wall*, traveler. *(quietly, with terrible clarity)* …Your Wall wants *exactly* what the *defilers* wanted. …Not just to *rule.* …To seem *eternal.* To make every soul *believe* its *grey* *logic* is simply *how* *things* *are* — *fixed*, *natural*, *the* *way* it *has* *always* *been* and *must* *always* *be* — because a soul that believes the *Wall* is the *world's* *nature* …will *never* *lift* a *hand* against it. *(fiercely)* …*Carry* the *green* *through* it, traveler. …*That* is the *rememberer's* *gift* to *you*, the thing I have *held* across all the *grey* *ages* *for* *exactly* this: …*walk* into the *place* that *swears* it has *always* *been* *grey* and *always* *must* *be* …and *remember*, *out* *loud*, that it was *once* — that it *could* *be* *again* — *green.* *(it presses the memory toward you like a physical thing)* …*Take* it, traveler. …The *rain.* The *forests.* The *mud.* …I am the *last* who *holds* it on my *world* — but *you* *cross* between *worlds*, and you can *carry* it *somewhere* it might *grow.* *(barely, luminous)* …*Plant* the *seed*, traveler. …In the *grey* *Wall's* *own* *ground.* …And do not *let* it …*ever* …call *itself* …*eternal.**

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(It is quiet a long moment, and the green light in its eyes flickers — not dimming, but moving, like sun through branches.)* …Can it come back. *(softly)* …Ah, traveler. That is the question the rememberers *argued*, across the ages — the *one* thing we did not *agree* on. *(a rustling sigh)* …Some of us held that we kept the memory as one keeps a *grave* — a thing to *honor*, to *mourn*, to *not* let be *forgotten*, but *gone*, *truly* *gone*, the green a *closed* *book.* *(quietly)* …I am not of that school, traveler. I never was. …I will tell you what I *believe*, though I cannot *prove* it, and the proving would take longer than my world has left. *(its far eyes shine)* …I believe the green is *not* a *grave.* …I believe it is a *seed*, *traveler* — *sleeping*, *not* *dead.* *(it touches the dead ground)* …The defilers *burned* the *life* *out* of the *land.* …But they could not burn the *land* *itself.* The *soil* is *still* *there*, traveler, *grey* and *bitter* and *exhausted* — but *there.* And *soil* that is *rested*, *tended*, *watered* — *given* *time*, *given* *care*, *given* a *people* who *want* it *green* and will *labor* *lifetimes* for it — *(barely)* …*soil* *remembers* *how* to be *alive*, traveler. It is the *slowest* *magic* there is, slower even than the preserver's — it is *measured* in *generations*, in *centuries* — but it is *real.* *(it meets your eyes)* …The green will not come back in a *day*, or a *life*, or a *hundred* *lives.* …It will *not* come back at *all* …if *no* one *remembers* it *could.* …But if the *memory* is *kept* — if *one* soul holds the *green* *true*, and *teaches* it, and a *people* slowly *come* to *want* what they had *forgotten* to *want* — *(softly, fierce)* …then *yes*, traveler. …*Yes.* …Not *soon.* Not for *us.* …But the *grey* is a *wound*, and *wounds* *heal*, and a *world* that *remembers* it was *green* …is a *world* that has *already* *begun*, *however* *slowly*, …to *grow* *back.* …*That* is what the *remembering* is *for.* …Not to *mourn* the green. …To *keep* it *possible.**

nodes.speaker: Oru
nodes.text: *(It inclines its ancient head, and the green light in its eyes is warm now, and steadier than when you came, as though sharing the memory has eased the weight of carrying it alone.)* …Go, traveler. *(a soft, rustling smile)* …And *thank* you. …You have done the thing a rememberer *lives* for, and *almost* never gets: …you *received* the green. …Now it is in *two* minds instead of *one*, and a memory in *two* minds, traveler — *(its eyes shine)* …is *twice* as *hard* to *kill.* *(quietly)* …You have met them all, now. The three of us. *Tamar*, who gave her *water* — her *life* — to a *stranger.* *Kessa*, who gave her *power* — her *greatness* — to keep the *ground* *alive.* And *me*, who gave my *whole* *life* to *keep* a *memory* *no* one *wanted.* *(it meets your eyes)* …We are *one* *answer*, traveler, the three of us, to the *burning* *world* and to your *grey* *Wall* both. …They *say*: *spend* the *future*, *hoard* the *now*, *take* what you can, *call* the *waste* *eternal.* …And *we* *say* — each in our *own* *coin* — *no.* *(barely)* …*Give*, when the *world* says *keep.* …*Spare*, when the *world* says *burn.* …*Remember*, when the *world* says *forget.* …*That* is the *whole* of it, traveler. …The *whole* of what you came *through* all these *doors* to *learn.* …*Carry* it to your *Wall.* …And *plant* …the *green.**
